metadata.title: Trilha
metadata.description: Starting college with hands-on experience and lasting relationships.

hero.lede: Trilha is a free program built by UFPB students to support people arriving at the Computing Center. Classes, mentoring, talks, and a hackathon — all in one semester.

sobre.title: More than a course. A network for life.
sobre.lede: Trilha started in 2024 as senior students' answer to a simple question: what was missing for us when we arrived? Today it is a program of classes, mentoring, talks, and events — built by people who walked the path already.

pillars.title: A curriculum aimed at industry
pillars.body: Python, web, data, and engineering fundamentals — taught by tutors already working in the field. Applied to a real project at the end of each semester.

pillars.title: Support that doesn't end with the class
pillars.body: Every student is paired with a senior mentor. The conversations continue long after the semester ends.

pillars.title: Guests from industry and research
pillars.body: Engineers, founders, and researchers bring back what is actually happening in the market and in academia.

pillars.title: Putting it all together at the end
pillars.body: Each cohort closes with a themed hackathon — teams build from scratch in a few days, the best way to consolidate everything learned.

turmas.title: Four semesters, four stories.
turmas.lede: Each cohort marks a version of Trilha. Meet the people who came through and what each edition built.

items.title: First cohort
items.theme: Where it all started — Python, web, and the first hackathon.

items.title: Second cohort
items.theme: Expanded curriculum, first wave of external guest speakers.

items.title: Third cohort
items.theme: Structured mentoring and a new data track.

items.title: Fourth cohort
items.theme: More cohorts, more projects, a community taking shape.

projects.title: What comes out of a Trilha semester.
projects.lede: Every semester ends with a themed hackathon. Teams build from scratch in a few days — below, some of the projects. (Links and details coming soon.)

items.title: ClarIAr
items.desc: Android accessibility app that uses LLMs to generate rich, natural descriptions of on-screen images, empowering visually impaired users.

items.title: Pixelmind
items.desc: AI-powered video editor: users upload a clip, describe the desired result, and the system automates the full edit.

items.title: Praxis
items.desc: Hands-on learning platform for junior devs with track-specific challenges and AI feedback. Simulate, practice, evolve.

depo.title: In the students' own words.
depo.lede: Excerpts from what alumni wrote about the experience.

time.title: The people making Trilha happen.
time.lede: Today the organization is made up of active tutors, former students who came back to help, and founders who stayed close.

materiais.title: Always open, always free.
materiais.lede: Trilha's teaching material — Python, frontend, backend, databases, data — stays freely available for anyone who wants to learn.

papers.title: Reading for those who want to go further.
papers.lede: Articles and research produced by the Trilha community.

items.title: Fullstack, from Zero
items.desc: A friendly article for newcomers on how frontend and backend connect and where to start.
items.author: Luigi Schmitt

items.title: A First Look at Machine Learning
items.desc: An introductory guide for those arriving in the world of ML — core concepts, intuition, and practical examples.
items.author: Malu Quintela

items.title: From Raw Data to Smart Decisions
items.desc: A journey from raw data to informed decisions — through EDA, modeling, and the intuition that connects both worlds.
items.author: Nicole Costa

items.title: What data hides before Machine Learning
items.desc: An interactive introduction to Exploratory Data Analysis and the questions that come before training a model.
items.author: Beatriz Cruz

items.title: Git, from zero
items.desc: A friendly version-control paper for those who hear commit, push, branch and just nod along.
items.author: Kruta

questions.a: Yes — open to students from any major, not restricted to UFPB only.
Registrations open periodically, when each cohort ends, at the start of each academic semester.

questions.q: How many cohorts run at once?
Do I need to know how to program?
questions.a: No. The program targets people starting out and assumes no prior experience.
One of the most frequently asked questions at Trilha, and Tiago's favorite color is

footer.tagline: Empowering and inspiring students for a bright future.

aulas.title: Trilha Classes.
aulas.lede: Access recordings and materials for each class of the semester. Have a question? Send a message directly to the team.

items.title: Python Basics 1
items.description: Variables, primitive types, operators, and first scripts. The foundation for writing any Python program.
items.homework: Write two programs: (1) one that reads the user's name, age, and city and prints a personalized greeting; (2) one that reads an integer and prints whether it is positive, negative, or zero. Push both .py files to a GitHub repository.

links.label: Python Tutor — visualize your code execution

items.title: Python Basics 2
items.description: Conditionals, loops, and control flow logic. How to structure a program's logic from start to finish.
items.homework: Write a function that calculates the factorial of a number using a loop. Then write a program that prints the first N terms of the Fibonacci sequence, where N is read from the user. Push both .py files to a GitHub repository.

items.title: Python Basics 3 — OOP
items.description: Object-oriented programming in practice: classes, attributes, methods, and inheritance. Modeling real problems with objects.

items.title: Python Basics 3 — Frameworks & Libraries
items.description: The Python ecosystem: pip, virtual environments, and the libraries every developer needs to know.
items.homework: Create a virtual environment with venv, install the requests library, and use it to fetch data from a public API (suggestion: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts). Print the title of the first 5 posts. Document the process in a README.md and push everything to GitHub.

items.title: Data 1 — Intro & Data Engineering
items.description: What data engineering is, pipelines, and how raw data becomes usable information in real systems.

items.title: Data 2 — Data Analysis
items.description: Exploration and visualization with pandas and matplotlib. Extracting patterns and insights from datasets.

items.title: Data 3 — ML
items.description: Introduction to Machine Learning: supervised models, training, testing, and how to evaluate performance in practice.

items.title: FullStack 1 — Backend
items.description: Building RESTful APIs with Python. Routes, requests, responses, and the logic that lives on the server.

items.title: FullStack 2 — Database
items.description: Relational modeling, SQL, and connecting a database to the backend. Data persistence in practice.

items.title: FullStack 3 — Frontend
items.description: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript applied: building interfaces that talk to the backend and make sense for the user.

form.submitLede: Send the link to your solution and any notes you think are relevant.
form.name: Name
form.successBody: We received your submission. The team will get back to you soon.
